articles.title: A Transcriptional Failure Leads to Systemic Inflammation
articles.summary: Researchers have found that bound pieces of RNA and DNA in the cytoplasm of senescent cells encourage these cells to secrete inflammatory factors. When transcription gets sticky As part of the transcription process that […]

articles.title: Amazon fish reveal a synchronized survival tactic that could transfer to drone swarms
articles.summary: Some fish swim in synchrony. Others, it turns out, breathe in synchrony. This is true for arapaimas, an obligate air-breathing species living in the Amazon. A new study in Communications Biology, led by the Leibniz Institute of Freshwater Ecology and Inland Fisheries (IGB) in collaboration with the Cluster of Excellence "Science of Intelligence," has demonstrated for the first time that arapaima juveniles gather by the hundreds to synchronize their trips to the water surface with split-second precision, most likely to avoid predators and maximize survival and efficiency.

articles.title: 'Super fungi' offer greener path to recovery of critical minerals
articles.summary: A "superpowered" fungus engineered at The University of Queensland could be used to extract critical minerals from toxic mining waste while also helping to remediate sites. Environmental engineers at UQ's new Biosustainability Hub are growing the unique fungal strains that can be used to detoxify mining tailings and capture traces of important rare earths without the need for harsh chemicals.

articles.title: First quantum biosensor can detect rapid, invisible changes in cells
articles.summary: In the development of diseases such as muscular dystrophy, cancer, Ebola and dengue, numerous chemical reactions take place within and between cells that contribute to disease progression. These changes can occur in less than the blink of an eye—within microseconds or faster—making them difficult to analyze. Quantum sensing offers a way to monitor these changes, helping researchers determine when, where and how rapid biological processes occur.

articles.title: Mapping and engineering the human cell–cell interactome
articles.summary: Nature Biotechnology, Published online: 23 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41587-026-03177-2 Di Carlo and colleagues discuss technologies required to map and engineer the human cell–cell interactome and the therapeutic avenues such an atlas could unlock.

articles.title: Efficient generation of epitope-targeted antibodies with Germinal
articles.summary: Nature Biotechnology, Published online: 23 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41587-026-03187-0 Germinal achieves epitope-targeted, de novo complementarity-determining region design with high experimental success rates.

articles.title: Leaf-based fluorescence test speeds search for plant gene-editing targets
articles.summary: Gene editing of plant DNA has the potential to produce crops with increased performance and resilience, but it can take a long time to achieve these gains. To shorten this process, scientists often use screening tools to determine where and how edits to the plant genome can be most effective.

articles.title: Bird-derived gene tool inserts plant DNA 30 times more efficiently than CRISPR
articles.summary: In a rapidly changing climate landscape, the plants we rely on for food, textiles and more face a multitude of challenges, including rising temperatures, drought and disease. Caltech's Gözde Demirer, the Clare Boothe Luce Assistant Professor of Chemical Engineering, uses genetic engineering tools to make crops more resilient to such threats and enhance plant health. Now, she and a team of Caltech researchers have found a new solution to an old problem in an unlikely source: the zebra finch.

articles.title: Protein-tagging technology maps a hidden communication network between organs
articles.summary: The body's organs are in constant communication. Fat tissue tells the liver when to store or release energy, the immune system signals localized inflammation, and thousands of proteins carry these messages to organs throughout the body. But while scientists have long known these conversations exist, they have struggled to identify exactly which cells are sending which messages.

articles.title: Researchers reveal the pathogenesis of a rare respiratory disease through super-resolution microscopy
articles.summary: Researchers at The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (HKUST) have uncovered how mutations in a specific gene can trigger a rare respiratory disease, shedding light on the critical role played by cellular structures known as cilia. Cilia are tiny hair-like organelles extruding from the surface of most cell types, serving either sensory or motile functions.

articles.title: CRISPR safety check evaluates intended and unintended mutations
articles.summary: A team of researchers led by Professor Akitsu Hotta (Department of Clinical Application) developed a comprehensive framework that combines computational prediction, experimental validation and whole-genome analysis to evaluate intended and unintended mutations arising from CRISPR-Cas9 delivered by lipid nanoparticles (LNPs), providing a practical strategy to improve the safety of genome-editing therapies. The work is published in the journal Molecular Therapy Nucleic Acids.

articles.title: Chainlink joins European and Korean bank consortia to develop FX settlement network
articles.summary: Banks across Europe and South Korea will study whether regulated euro and won stablecoins can enable real-time cross-border foreign exchange settlement.

articles.title: Chainlink teams up with 47 South Korean, European banks to speed up international money transfers
articles.summary: The alliance, called Project Pangea, plans to use stablecoins to settle multimillion-dollar currency trades between Europe and South Korea in near real time.

articles.title: Tether-backed Oobit brings USDT to nearly 170 million users of Brazil’s PIX payment network
articles.summary: Created by Banco Central do Brasil in 2020, PIX is one of the world's most widely-adopted digital payment networks.

articles.title: Bitcoin teases $62K breakdown as analysis sees Micron earnings volatility next
articles.summary: Bitcoin stayed volatile while bulls tried to preserve support at local lows while stocks prepared for Micron forward earnings and digested Asia losses.

articles.title: Vitalik Buterin says Ethereum Foundation will cut budget 40% in major reset
articles.summary: The reduction comes on the same day the EF confirmed a 20% reduction in headcount and follows the resignation of co-Executive Director Hsiao-Wei Wang. Her departure brings the total number of senior Ethereum Foundation figures to leave since January to nine, underscoring the scale of the organization's ongoing turmoil.

articles.title: Bitcoin's recent drop below $60,000 signals Fed, ETF and AI pressures: Deutsche Bank
articles.summary: Bitcoin's slump to its lowest level since late 2024 reflects a hawkish Federal Reserve, exchange-traded fund outflows and a shift of capital into AI, the bank said.

articles.title: Bitcoin slump worsens amid SpaceX rout: Can BTC price hold $60K any longer?
articles.summary: SpaceX’s $600 billion wipeout rattles tech markets, putting Bitcoin’s $60,000 support at risk as traders brace for a deeper BTC selloff.

articles.title: Why tokenized SpaceX shares broke before retail investors could buy them
articles.summary: Tokenized SpaceX shares drew more than $1 billion in demand, but many investors received refunds instead. What went wrong?

articles.title: The SEC delayed tokenizing stocks, and here’s why that’s a relief
articles.summary: Before we rush to tokenize U.S. equities, we need compliance systems that understand context, not just checkboxes. Investors like Michael Burry see the danger, so should the rest of us, urges Chamarajnagar.

articles.title: Ethereum Foundation cuts 20% of its workforce as new 5-cluster structure takes shape
articles.summary: Ethereum Foundation cut 20% of its workforce, reorganizing into five clusters as part of a long-simmering reorganization.
